nativeRange.habitat: Muddy and sandy bottoms of estuaries, bays, and coastal shallows; occasionally found in seagrass areas. Generally demersal, occurring from the intertidal zone to roughly 73 m depth, most commonly between 9 and 27 m. Adults tend to inhabit muddy estuaries and have been observed partly buried in substrate.
nativeRange.biotope: Sand and mud flats in coastal estuaries and bays

decorPreferences: open substrate footprint with minimal rockwork
mature live rock or live sand with established copepod and amphipod populations
refugium or live sand bed with abundant meiofauna
scattered seagrass or macroalgae for cover

diet.notes: In the wild, has been described as picking small benthic invertebrates — primarily small crustaceans, copepods, and amphipods — directly from the substrate surface. In captivity, has been reported to require live micro-crustaceans on arrival (live enriched brine shrimp, live copepods); finely cut meaty foods and vitamin-enriched frozen brine or mysis shrimp may eventually become acceptable for some specimens, but many individuals are widely documented as refusing prepared foods. Multiple small feedings per day at or near the substrate are generally advised. Starvation has been reported as a common cause of loss in captivity.

avoidWith: any species that will outcompete it for food

summary: The Flying Jet Sea Moth (Pegasus volitans) is a slow-moving, bottom-dwelling fish from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its flattened, bony-plated body and broad, wing-like pectoral fins. It is widely considered expert-only, owing to a strong dependence on live foods and its vulnerability to competition at feeding time.
careNotes: The Flying Jet Sea Moth is generally advised to be kept in a mature, peaceful aquarium with a fine sand or silt substrate, a substantial open substrate footprint, abundant meiofauna (tiny crustaceans such as copepods and amphipods that colonise live sand), and low to moderate flow. As a slow, methodical bottom-feeder that tends to pick small invertebrates directly off the substrate, it is easily outcompeted by faster-moving tankmates, and multiple small feedings per day at or near the substrate are typically required. The older scientific name Parapegasus natans remains in wide aquarium-trade use, but current ichthyology treats it as a junior synonym of Pegasus volitans Linnaeus, 1758; the genus Parapegasus itself is now folded into Pegasus. The Dragon Sea Moth ([sw-fish-327]) is a distinct and smaller related species in the same family, reaching only around 7–10 cm, and should not be confused with this species. The Flying Jet Sea Moth is rarely available in the trade and is typically wild-caught; captive breeding has not been documented.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form pairs in the wild, with adults inhabiting muddy estuaries during the breeding season. Captive breeding in home aquaria has not been meaningfully documented; offspring are not available in the trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level (Expert Only), peaceful temperament, reef-with-caution status, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 50 US gal minimum tank, diet (live micro-crustaceans found in live rock; finely cut meaty foods and vitamin-enriched brine/mysis may become acceptable), Indo-Pacific origin. Scientific name listed as Parapegasus natans. Maximum size stated as 8 inches (~20 cm).

additional.name: FishBase — Pegasus volitans
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pegasidae, Syngnathiformes), maximum size (20 cm TL), temperature range (23.8–29.1°C per environmental model), depth range (1–73 m, typically 9–27 m), demersal habitat on sandy/muddy bottoms and estuaries, pair-forming behaviour in adults, and benthic feeding. FishBase resolves Parapegasus natans as a synonym of Pegasus volitans.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pegasus volitans
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range countries (Australia, Bahrain, China, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Mozambique, Myanmar, Philippines, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, Taiwan, Tanzania, Thailand), confirmed Parapegasus natans as an explicit synonym, and noted the species' use in the aquarium trade. Family Pegasidae, Order Syngnathiformes confirmed. IUCN status: Data Deficient (2016).

additional.name: ITIS
additional.notes: Confirmed Parapegasus natans is an 'invalid — junior synonym' of Pegasus volitans Linnaeus, 1758 (TSN 167637). Informs the scientificName choice and alsoKnownAs list. Common name given as 'longtailed sea dragon'. Designated secondary source (Reef2Reef thread) returned HTTP 403; ITIS substituted as additional taxonomy reference per playbook fallback rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported to form pairs in the wild during the breeding season. Captive breeding in home aquaria has not been meaningfully documented; offspring are not available in the trade.
